name: The D.H. Holmes clock, Canal Street
certaintyNote: The novel opens by name at the clock outside the D.H. Holmes department store on Canal Street; the store closed in 1989 and the building is now a hotel, with a statue of the hero marking the spot at 819 Canal.
note: The book begins with the hero loitering under a landmark department-store clock, waiting for his mother and sneering at the crowd of shoppers around him. The store is long gone, redeveloped into a hotel, but a bronze figure in a hunting cap now stands under a replica clock to mark where the comedy starts.

sources.ref: Ignatius J. Reilly Statue, Atlas Obscura, on the clock and the former D.H. Holmes store at 819 Canal Street
Blakeview: Remembering D.H. Holmes, Gambit / nola.com, on the store's Canal Street history

name: The Reilly house, Constantinople Street
certaintyNote: Toole names Constantinople Street in Uptown but never gives a number; local research points to the modest shotgun houses in the 600 block of the Touro neighbourhood, so this marks that block.
note: The hero lives with his widowed mother in a cramped house on a quiet Uptown street, holed up in a back room writing screeds against the modern age. The author names the street but no address; the humble shotgun houses in the 600 block have long been read as the model for the home.

sources.ref: A million bucks for Ignatius Reilly's New Orleans house?, nola.com, on the 600 block of Constantinople Street in Touro
The Reilly House, Ignatius' Ghost, on the Constantinople Street setting in the Touro district

name: The Night of Joy, Bourbon Street
certaintyNote: The Night of Joy bar is invented, but the novel plants it explicitly on Bourbon Street in the French Quarter, so this marks a representative point on the strip of neon and clubs.
note: A seedy Bourbon Street nightclub is the engine of half the plot, with its put-upon barmaid, its scheming owner, and a stripper whose act involves a cockatoo. The bar itself is fiction, but the author sets it firmly on the real Bourbon Street, amid the drink and neon the hero loudly deplores.

sources.ref: 1980: A Confederacy of Dunces holds up a mirror to New Orleans, nola.com, on the novel's French Quarter and Bourbon Street settings
Bourbon Street, Wikipedia, for the French Quarter location

name: Levy Pants, down by the river
certaintyNote: Levy Pants is fictional; the novel places it downriver by the Industrial Canal near Poland Avenue, in what is now Bywater, so this marks that riverside stretch. No surviving building matches the described factory.
note: The hero takes a clerical job at a shabby, failing trouser factory downriver, where he promptly launches a doomed workers' crusade and files the correspondence in the bin. The author sites the plant by the river near the Industrial Canal, in the working district now called Bywater.

sources.ref: Levy Pants, Inc., Ignatius' Ghost, on the factory's setting by the Industrial Canal and river in Bywater
Bywater, New Orleans, Wikipedia, for the downriver district near the Industrial Canal

name: The French Quarter hot-dog rounds
certaintyNote: The novel sets the hero's vending in the French Quarter without fixing one corner, since the cart roams, so this marks the heart of the Quarter at Jackson Square.
note: Cast out to earn a living, the hero pushes a hot-dog cart through the French Quarter, eating most of the stock himself and costumed as a pirate to hawk them. The cart wanders the old streets around Jackson Square, the tourist heart of the Quarter, which anchors this stop.

sources.ref: Lucky Dogs, 64 Parishes, on the real French Quarter hot-dog vendors behind the novel's cart
Jackson Square, Wikipedia, for the French Quarter coordinates
